concepts.name: SI Units and Measurement
concepts.description: The International System of Units defines seven base units used to express all physical quantities.

concepts.name: Scalar and Vector Quantities
concepts.description: Scalars have magnitude only while vectors have both magnitude and direction.

concepts.name: Dimensional Analysis
concepts.description: Dimensional analysis checks the consistency of equations by examining the dimensions of each term.

concepts.name: Measurement Errors and Uncertainty
concepts.description: Measurement errors quantify the deviation of a measured value from the true value.

concepts.name: Significant Figures and Precision
concepts.description: Significant figures indicate the precision of a measurement by specifying which digits are meaningful.

concepts.name: Vector Addition and Resolution
concepts.description: Vectors are added by the parallelogram law or by resolving them into orthogonal components.

concepts.name: Dot and Cross Products
concepts.description: The dot product yields a scalar and the cross product yields a vector perpendicular to both operands.

concepts.name: Unit Conversion and Prefixes
concepts.description: Unit conversion uses multiplicative factors or SI prefixes to express quantities in different units.

concepts.name: Displacement and Distance
concepts.description: Displacement is the vector change in position while distance is the total scalar path length.

concepts.name: Speed and Velocity
concepts.description: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with time; speed is its scalar counterpart.

concepts.name: Acceleration
concepts.description: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time.

concepts.name: Kinematics in One Dimension
concepts.description: Kinematic equations relate displacement, velocity, acceleration and time for uniform acceleration in one dimension.

concepts.name: Kinematics in Two Dimensions
concepts.description: Two-dimensional kinematics applies vector decomposition to analyse motion in a plane.

concepts.name: Projectile Motion
concepts.description: Projectile motion combines uniform horizontal velocity with uniformly accelerated vertical motion under gravity.

concepts.name: Relative Motion and Reference Frames
concepts.description: Relative motion describes the velocity and position of one object as observed from a moving reference frame.

concepts.name: Uniform Circular Motion
concepts.description: Uniform circular motion involves constant speed along a circular path with centripetal acceleration directed toward the centre.

concepts.name: Force and Interaction
concepts.description: A force is a vector quantity representing an interaction that can change an object's state of motion.

concepts.name: Newton's First Law — Inertia
concepts.description: An object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net external force.

concepts.name: Newton's Second Law — F=ma
concepts.description: The net force on a body equals the product of its mass and acceleration: F = ma.

concepts.name: Newton's Third Law — Action-Reaction
concepts.description: For every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force acting on a different body.

concepts.name: Free Body Diagrams
concepts.description: A free body diagram isolates a body and represents all external forces acting on it as vectors.

concepts.name: Friction Forces
concepts.description: Friction is a contact force that opposes relative motion between surfaces in contact.

concepts.name: Tension in Strings and Ropes
concepts.description: Tension is the pulling force transmitted through a string or rope connecting objects.

concepts.name: Normal Force and Constraint Forces
concepts.description: The normal force is the perpendicular contact force exerted by a surface on an object resting on it.

concepts.name: Motion on Inclined Planes
concepts.description: Motion on an inclined plane is analysed by resolving gravitational and normal forces along and perpendicular to the slope.

concepts.name: Work Done by a Force
concepts.description: Work is the scalar product of force and displacement, measuring energy transfer by a force.

concepts.name: Kinetic Energy
concepts.description: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by an object due to its motion, equal to ½mv².

concepts.name: Potential Energy
concepts.description: Potential energy is stored energy associated with an object's position in a conservative force field.

concepts.name: Work-Energy Theorem
concepts.description: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy.

concepts.name: Conservation of Mechanical Energy
concepts.description: In the absence of non-conservative forces, the total mechanical energy of a system remains constant.

concepts.name: Power
concepts.description: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts.

concepts.name: Conservative and Non-Conservative Forces
concepts.description: Conservative forces do path-independent work and have associated potential energies; non-conservative forces dissipate energy.

concepts.name: Linear Momentum
concepts.description: Linear momentum is the product of mass and velocity, a conserved vector quantity in isolated systems.

concepts.name: Impulse and Momentum Change
concepts.description: Impulse is the product of force and time interval and equals the change in momentum of the object.

concepts.name: Conservation of Linear Momentum
concepts.description: The total linear momentum of an isolated system is conserved when no net external force acts on it.

concepts.name: Elastic Collisions
concepts.description: In an elastic collision both linear momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

concepts.name: Inelastic Collisions
concepts.description: In an inelastic collision momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not; in perfectly inelastic collisions the bodies stick together.

concepts.name: Center of Mass
concepts.description: The center of mass is the mass-weighted average position of a system, which moves as if all external forces acted there.

concepts.name: Angular Kinematics
concepts.description: Angular kinematics describes rotational motion using angular displacement, velocity, and acceleration analogous to linear kinematics.

concepts.name: Torque
concepts.description: Torque is the rotational equivalent of force, equal to the cross product of the position vector and force.

concepts.name: Moment of Inertia
concepts.description: Moment of inertia quantifies a body's resistance to angular acceleration and depends on mass distribution about the rotation axis.

concepts.name: Rotational Dynamics
concepts.description: Rotational dynamics relates net torque to angular acceleration via τ = Iα, the rotational form of Newton's second law.

concepts.name: Angular Momentum
concepts.description: Angular momentum is the rotational analogue of linear momentum, equal to the product of moment of inertia and angular velocity.

concepts.name: Conservation of Angular Momentum
concepts.description: The total angular momentum of an isolated system remains constant when no net external torque acts on it.

concepts.name: Rolling Without Slipping
concepts.description: Rolling without slipping combines translation and rotation such that the contact point has zero instantaneous velocity.

concepts.name: Static Equilibrium
concepts.description: A body is in static equilibrium when the net force and net torque acting on it are both zero.

concepts.name: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
concepts.description: Every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of their separation.

concepts.name: Gravitational Field and Field Lines
concepts.description: The gravitational field at a point is the gravitational force per unit mass experienced by a test mass placed there.

concepts.name: Gravitational Potential Energy
concepts.description: Gravitational potential energy is the work done against gravity to bring a mass from infinity to a given point.

concepts.name: Circular Orbital Mechanics
concepts.description: Circular orbital mechanics balances gravitational attraction with centripetal acceleration to determine orbital speed and period.

concepts.name: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
concepts.description: Kepler's three laws describe elliptical orbits, equal-area sweeping, and the period-radius relationship for planetary motion.

concepts.name: Escape Velocity
concepts.description: Escape velocity is the minimum speed needed for an object to escape a gravitational field without further propulsion.

concepts.name: Artificial Satellites and Geostationary Orbits
concepts.description: Artificial satellites orbit Earth with periods determined by altitude; geostationary satellites match Earth's rotation period.

concepts.name: Hooke's Law and Spring Force
concepts.description: Hooke's law states that the restoring force of a spring is proportional to its displacement from equilibrium: F = −kx.

concepts.name: Stress, Strain, and Elastic Moduli
concepts.description: Stress is force per unit area; strain is fractional deformation; their ratio defines the elastic modulus of a material.

concepts.name: Pressure in Fluids
concepts.description: Pressure in a static fluid increases with depth and acts equally in all directions (Pascal's principle).

concepts.name: Buoyancy and Archimedes' Principle
concepts.description: Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of fluid displaced.

concepts.name: Bernoulli's Equation and Fluid Flow
concepts.description: Bernoulli's equation expresses conservation of energy in steady, incompressible fluid flow along a streamline.

concepts.name: Surface Tension and Capillarity
concepts.description: Surface tension is the energy per unit area at a liquid surface arising from cohesive intermolecular forces.

concepts.name: Viscosity and Stokes' Law
concepts.description: Viscosity measures a fluid's internal resistance to flow; Stokes' law gives the drag force on a sphere moving through a viscous fluid.

concepts.name: Generalized Coordinates and Configuration Space
concepts.description: Generalized coordinates are a minimal set of independent parameters that uniquely specify the configuration of a mechanical system subject to holonomic constraints; the number of independent coordinates equals the degrees of freedom.

concepts.name: The Euler-Lagrange Equation and Hamilton's Principle
concepts.description: Hamilton's principle states that the action S = ∫L dt is stationary for the physical path; varying S yields the Euler-Lagrange equation d/dt(∂L/∂ṡᵢ) − ∂L/∂qᵢ = 0, where L = T − V is the Lagrangian.

concepts.name: Cyclic Coordinates and Noether's Theorem
concepts.description: A generalized coordinate is cyclic if ∂L/∂qᵢ = 0; the conjugate momentum pᵢ = ∂L/∂ṡᵢ is then conserved. Noether's theorem generalises this: every continuous symmetry of the Lagrangian implies a conserved quantity.

concepts.name: The Hamiltonian and Legendre Transform
concepts.description: The Hamiltonian H = Σpᵢṡᵢ − L is obtained from the Lagrangian by a Legendre transform; canonical momenta pᵢ = ∂L/∂ṡᵢ are conjugate to the generalised coordinates, and H equals the total energy for natural systems.

concepts.name: Hamilton's Equations of Motion
concepts.description: Hamilton's equations ṡᵢ = ∂H/∂pᵢ and ṗᵢ = −∂H/∂qᵢ give 2N coupled first-order equations governing trajectories in phase space, equivalent to the Euler-Lagrange equations but symmetric in q and p.

concepts.name: Poisson Brackets and Phase Space Dynamics
concepts.description: The Poisson bracket {f,g} = Σ(∂f/∂qᵢ ∂g/∂pᵢ − ∂f/∂pᵢ ∂g/∂qᵢ) encodes dynamics: df/dt = {f,H}. Fundamental brackets {qᵢ,pⱼ} = δᵢⱼ prefigure quantum commutators; Liouville's theorem states phase-space volume is conserved.

concepts.name: Canonical Transformations and Generating Functions
concepts.description: Canonical transformations (Qᵢ, Pᵢ) = (Qᵢ(q,p,t), Pᵢ(q,p,t)) preserve Hamilton's equations and Poisson bracket structure; they are generated by four types of generating functions F₁–F₄ that mix coordinates and momenta.

concepts.name: Hamilton-Jacobi Equation and Action-Angle Variables
concepts.description: The Hamilton-Jacobi equation −∂S/∂t = H(q, ∂S/∂q, t) reduces mechanics to a single PDE for Hamilton's principal function S; separation of variables yields action-angle variables (J,θ) for integrable systems and is the classical precursor to the Schrödinger equation.

concepts.name: Temperature and Thermal Equilibrium
concepts.description: Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles; thermal equilibrium exists when two bodies in contact have equal temperatures.

concepts.name: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.description: If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

concepts.name: Thermal Expansion
concepts.description: Thermal expansion describes how solids, liquids, and gases change dimensions with temperature.

concepts.name: Heat Transfer: Conduction, Convection, Radiation
concepts.description: Heat transfers between systems by conduction (contact), convection (fluid flow), or radiation (electromagnetic waves).

concepts.name: Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.description: Specific heat capacity is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a substance by one degree.

concepts.name: Calorimetry
concepts.description: Calorimetry measures heat exchange using the principle that heat lost by a hot body equals heat gained by a cold body.

concepts.name: Phase Transitions and Latent Heat
concepts.description: Phase transitions absorb or release latent heat at constant temperature as a substance changes between solid, liquid, and gas phases.

concepts.name: Ideal Gas Law
concepts.description: The ideal gas law PV = nRT relates pressure, volume, amount, and absolute temperature of an ideal gas.

concepts.name: Kinetic Theory of Gases
concepts.description: Kinetic theory derives macroscopic gas properties from the statistical mechanics of molecular motion.

concepts.name: First Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.description: The first law states that heat added to a system equals the increase in internal energy plus the work done by the system: ΔU = Q − W.

concepts.name: Internal Energy of a System
concepts.description: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of all molecules in a thermodynamic system.

concepts.name: Thermodynamic Processes
concepts.description: Thermodynamic processes (isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric) describe how state variables change when constraints are applied.

concepts.name: Second Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.description: The second law states that heat spontaneously flows from hot to cold, and no engine can be 100% efficient.

concepts.name: Entropy and Disorder
concepts.description: Entropy is a state function measuring the dispersal of energy; it increases in all irreversible processes.

concepts.name: Heat Engines and Efficiency
concepts.description: A heat engine converts part of the heat absorbed from a hot reservoir into work while rejecting heat to a cold reservoir.

concepts.name: Carnot Cycle
concepts.description: The Carnot cycle is the most efficient possible heat engine cycle operating between two fixed temperature reservoirs.

concepts.name: Refrigerators and Heat Pumps
concepts.description: A refrigerator uses work to transfer heat from a cold reservoir to a hot reservoir; the coefficient of performance measures its efficiency.

concepts.name: Third Law of Thermodynamics
concepts.description: The third law states that the entropy of a perfect crystal approaches zero as temperature approaches absolute zero.

concepts.name: Simple Harmonic Motion
concepts.description: Simple harmonic motion is periodic oscillation in which the restoring force is proportional to and directed opposite the displacement.

concepts.name: Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion
concepts.description: In SHM the total mechanical energy remains constant while kinetic and potential energies interchange sinusoidally.

concepts.name: Simple Pendulum
concepts.description: A simple pendulum undergoes approximate SHM for small angles with period T = 2π√(L/g).

concepts.name: Spring-Mass System
concepts.description: A mass on a spring executes SHM with angular frequency ω = √(k/m) and period T = 2π√(m/k).

concepts.name: Damped Oscillations
concepts.description: Damped oscillations occur when a resistive force causes the amplitude to decrease exponentially with time.

concepts.name: Forced Oscillations and Resonance
concepts.description: Forced oscillations occur when an external periodic force drives a system; resonance is maximum amplitude when the driving frequency matches the natural frequency.

concepts.name: Wave Properties: Amplitude, Period, Frequency
concepts.description: Waves are characterised by amplitude, wavelength, period, frequency, and phase, related by v = fλ.

concepts.name: Transverse Waves
concepts.description: In transverse waves the particle displacement is perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.

concepts.name: Longitudinal Waves
concepts.description: In longitudinal waves the particle displacement is parallel to the direction of wave propagation, forming compressions and rarefactions.

concepts.name: Wave Speed and the Wave Equation
concepts.description: Wave speed is determined by the medium's properties; the wave equation relates displacement to position and time.

concepts.name: Superposition Principle
concepts.description: The superposition principle states that the resultant displacement is the algebraic sum of displacements of individual waves.

concepts.name: Wave Interference
concepts.description: Wave interference is the superposition of two or more waves producing regions of reinforcement or cancellation.

concepts.name: Standing Waves
concepts.description: Standing waves form when two identical waves travel in opposite directions, producing fixed nodes and antinodes.

concepts.name: Sound Waves
concepts.description: Sound waves are longitudinal mechanical waves that propagate through a medium via pressure fluctuations.

concepts.name: Sound Intensity and the Decibel Scale
concepts.description: Sound intensity is power per unit area; the decibel scale logarithmically measures intensity relative to a reference level.

concepts.name: Doppler Effect
concepts.description: The Doppler effect is the change in observed frequency due to relative motion between source and observer.

concepts.name: Beats and Beat Frequency
concepts.description: Beats are periodic variations in amplitude produced by the superposition of two waves of slightly different frequencies.

concepts.name: Nature of Light: Ray and Wave Models
concepts.description: Light exhibits both ray (geometric) and wave (physical) behaviour depending on the scale of the optical phenomena.

concepts.name: Reflection and Laws of Reflection
concepts.description: The law of reflection states that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection with both angles measured from the normal.

concepts.name: Spherical Mirrors and Mirror Formula
concepts.description: The mirror formula 1/f = 1/v + 1/u relates focal length, image distance, and object distance for spherical mirrors.

concepts.name: Refraction and Snell's Law
concepts.description: Snell's law n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂ describes how light bends at an interface between media of different refractive indices.

concepts.name: Total Internal Reflection and Critical Angle
concepts.description: Total internal reflection occurs when light hits a boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle and all light is reflected.

concepts.name: Thin Lenses and Lens Formula
concepts.description: The thin lens formula 1/f = 1/v − 1/u relates focal length, image distance, and object distance for thin lenses.

concepts.name: Power of a Lens and Lens Combinations
concepts.description: The power of a lens is the reciprocal of focal length in metres; combined lenses have powers that add algebraically.

concepts.name: Optical Instruments: Eye, Microscope, Telescope
concepts.description: Optical instruments use combinations of lenses and mirrors to magnify images for the human eye.

concepts.name: Dispersion and Prisms
concepts.description: Dispersion is the separation of white light into its constituent wavelengths by a prism due to wavelength-dependent refractive index.

concepts.name: Introduction to Wave Optics
concepts.description: Wave optics treats light as a wave and uses Huygens' principle to explain interference and diffraction phenomena.

concepts.name: Young's Double-Slit Experiment
concepts.description: Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates light interference producing a pattern of alternating bright and dark fringes.

concepts.name: Diffraction of Light
concepts.description: Diffraction is the bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through apertures, observable when the aperture size is comparable to wavelength.

concepts.name: Single-Slit Diffraction
concepts.description: Single-slit diffraction produces a central maximum flanked by progressively weaker secondary maxima when light passes through a narrow slit.

concepts.name: Polarization of Light
concepts.description: Polarization describes the orientation of the electric field oscillation in transverse electromagnetic waves.

concepts.name: Brewster's Law and Malus's Law
concepts.description: Brewster's law gives the angle at which reflected light is completely polarized; Malus's law relates transmitted intensity to analyser angle.

concepts.name: Electric Charge and Conservation
concepts.description: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that is quantised and conserved in all interactions.

concepts.name: Coulomb's Law
concepts.description: Coulomb's law states that the electrostatic force between two point charges is proportional to their product and inversely proportional to the square of their separation.

concepts.name: Electric Field and Field Lines
concepts.description: The electric field at a point is the electrostatic force per unit positive test charge placed at that point.

concepts.name: Electric Dipole
concepts.description: An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance; it experiences torque in a uniform field.

concepts.name: Gauss's Law
concepts.description: Gauss's law relates the total electric flux through a closed surface to the enclosed charge: Φ = Q_enc/ε₀.

concepts.name: Electric Potential and Potential Difference
concepts.description: Electric potential is the work done per unit charge to bring a positive test charge from infinity to a given point.

concepts.name: Capacitance and Capacitors
concepts.description: Capacitance measures a capacitor's ability to store electric charge; C = Q/V.

concepts.name: Dielectrics and Polarization
concepts.description: Dielectrics are insulators that become polarized in an electric field, increasing capacitance by reducing the internal field.

concepts.name: Energy Stored in a Capacitor
concepts.description: The energy stored in a charged capacitor is U = ½CV² = Q²/2C, stored in the electric field between the plates.

concepts.name: Electric Current and Drift Velocity
concepts.description: Electric current is the rate of flow of charge; drift velocity is the average velocity of charge carriers under an electric field.

concepts.name: Ohm's Law and Resistance
concepts.description: Ohm's law states that the current through a conductor is proportional to the voltage across it: V = IR.

concepts.name: Resistivity and Conductivity
concepts.description: Resistivity is a material property relating resistance to geometry; conductivity is its reciprocal.

concepts.name: Series and Parallel Circuits
concepts.description: In series circuits the same current flows through all components; in parallel circuits the same voltage appears across each branch.

concepts.name: Kirchhoff's Current and Voltage Laws
concepts.description: Kirchhoff's current law states charge conservation at junctions; Kirchhoff's voltage law states energy conservation around loops.

concepts.name: Wheatstone Bridge
concepts.description: The Wheatstone bridge is a circuit for precise measurement of an unknown resistance by balancing four resistances.

concepts.name: Potentiometer
concepts.description: A potentiometer measures EMF and internal resistance by comparing potential differences along a uniform resistance wire.

concepts.name: Electrical Power and Joule Heating
concepts.description: Electrical power is the rate of energy dissipation; Joule heating P = I²R converts electrical energy to heat in a resistor.

concepts.name: EMF, Internal Resistance, and Terminal Voltage
concepts.description: EMF is the energy supplied per unit charge by a source; terminal voltage is reduced by the voltage drop across internal resistance.

concepts.name: RC Circuits: Charging and Discharging
concepts.description: In an RC circuit the capacitor charges and discharges exponentially with time constant τ = RC.

concepts.name: Magnetic Field and Field Lines
concepts.description: A magnetic field exerts forces on moving charges and current-carrying conductors; field lines indicate its direction and strength.

concepts.name: Magnetic Force on Moving Charges — Lorentz Force
concepts.description: The Lorentz force F = q(E + v × B) acts on a charge q moving with velocity v in electric and magnetic fields.

concepts.name: Biot-Savart Law
concepts.description: The Biot-Savart law gives the infinitesimal magnetic field contribution dB from a current element Id𝑙.

concepts.name: Ampere's Circuital Law
concepts.description: Ampere's law relates the line integral of magnetic field around a closed loop to the enclosed current.

concepts.name: Magnetic Field of a Solenoid and Toroid
concepts.description: The solenoid produces a uniform axial magnetic field B = μ₀nI inside; a toroid confines the field within its core.

concepts.name: Dia-, Para-, and Ferromagnetism
concepts.description: Materials are classified as diamagnetic, paramagnetic, or ferromagnetic based on their response to external magnetic fields.

concepts.name: Magnetic Dipole and Earth's Magnetism
concepts.description: A magnetic dipole has a moment m = NIA; Earth behaves as a giant magnetic dipole with geographic and magnetic poles.

concepts.name: Magnetic Flux
concepts.description: Magnetic flux Φ = B·A is the total magnetic field passing perpendicularly through a surface area A.

concepts.name: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction
concepts.description: Faraday's law states that the induced EMF equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux through a circuit: ε = −dΦ/dt.

concepts.name: Lenz's Law and Induced EMF Direction
concepts.description: Lenz's law states that the direction of induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux that caused it.

concepts.name: Self-Inductance
concepts.description: Self-inductance measures a coil's ability to oppose changes in its own current by inducing a back-EMF: ε = −L dI/dt.

concepts.name: Mutual Inductance and Transformers
concepts.description: Mutual inductance describes the EMF induced in one coil by a changing current in another; transformers use this principle to change voltage levels.

concepts.name: Alternating Current: Peak and RMS Values
concepts.description: Alternating current varies sinusoidally with time; RMS values equal peak values divided by √2 for power calculations.

concepts.name: LC Oscillations and Resonance
concepts.description: An LC circuit oscillates at resonant frequency f = 1/(2π√LC) as energy alternates between inductor and capacitor.

concepts.name: Maxwell's Equations and Displacement Current
concepts.description: Maxwell's four equations unify electricity and magnetism and predict electromagnetic wave propagation through displacement current.

concepts.name: Electromagnetic Waves and the EM Spectrum
concepts.description: Electromagnetic waves are self-sustaining oscillations of electric and magnetic fields that propagate at the speed of light.

concepts.name: Photoelectric Effect
concepts.description: The photoelectric effect is the emission of electrons from a metal surface when light of sufficient frequency falls on it, explained by photon quantisation.

concepts.name: Photons and Quantization of Light
concepts.description: Light is quantised into photons each carrying energy E = hf, explaining phenomena that classical wave theory cannot.

concepts.name: Compton Scattering
concepts.description: Compton scattering is the increase in wavelength of X-rays scattered by electrons, confirming the particle nature of photons.

concepts.name: de Broglie Hypothesis — Matter Waves
concepts.description: De Broglie proposed that every moving particle has an associated wavelength λ = h/p, unifying wave and particle pictures.

concepts.name: Wave-Particle Duality
concepts.description: Wave-particle duality is the principle that quantum objects exhibit both wave and particle properties depending on the experiment.

concepts.name: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
concepts.description: Bohr's model of hydrogen assumes quantised circular orbits, giving discrete energy levels En = −13.6/n² eV.

concepts.name: Atomic Spectra and Energy Levels
concepts.description: Atomic spectra arise from electrons transitioning between discrete energy levels, emitting or absorbing photons of specific frequencies.

concepts.name: X-Rays and Their Properties
concepts.description: X-rays are high-energy electromagnetic radiation produced by accelerating electrons into a metal target, used in medical imaging and crystallography.

concepts.name: Radioactivity: Alpha, Beta, Gamma Decay
concepts.description: Radioactivity is the spontaneous emission of alpha particles, beta particles, or gamma rays from unstable atomic nuclei.

concepts.name: Radioactive Decay Law and Half-Life
concepts.description: Radioactive decay follows an exponential law N = N₀e^(−λt); the half-life is the time for half the nuclei to decay.

concepts.name: Nuclear Reactions and Q-Value
concepts.description: Nuclear reactions conserve charge, mass number, and energy; the Q-value is the energy released or absorbed.

concepts.name: Nuclear Binding Energy and Mass Defect
concepts.description: Nuclear binding energy is the energy equivalent of the mass defect — the difference between free nucleon masses and the nucleus mass.

concepts.name: Nuclear Fission and Chain Reactions
concepts.description: Nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus into lighter fragments releasing enormous energy; a chain reaction sustains continuous fission.

concepts.name: Nuclear Fusion and Stellar Energy
concepts.description: Nuclear fusion combines light nuclei into heavier ones releasing energy; it powers stars and is the basis of hydrogen bombs.

concepts.name: Nuclear Models: Shell Model
concepts.description: The nuclear shell model explains magic numbers and nuclear stability by assigning nucleons to quantised energy shells.

concepts.name: Wave Function and Probability Interpretation
concepts.description: The wave function ψ is a complex amplitude whose squared modulus gives the probability density of finding a particle at a location.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.1

concepts.name: Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation
concepts.description: The Schrödinger equation iℏ∂ψ/∂t = Ĥψ governs the time evolution of the quantum wave function.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.1

concepts.name: Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle
concepts.description: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2, setting a fundamental limit on simultaneous measurement precision.

concepts.name: Quantum Operators and Observables
concepts.description: Physical observables in quantum mechanics are represented by Hermitian operators whose eigenvalues give measurement outcomes.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.3

concepts.name: Particle in an Infinite Square Well
concepts.description: The infinite square well is an exactly solvable quantum model giving quantised energies En = n²π²ℏ²/(2mL²).
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.2

concepts.name: Quantum Harmonic Oscillator
concepts.description: The quantum harmonic oscillator has equally spaced energy levels En = (n+½)ℏω, solved by ladder operators.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.2

concepts.name: Quantum Treatment of Hydrogen Atom
concepts.description: The quantum mechanical hydrogen atom gives four quantum numbers (n, l, m, s) and exact wavefunctions via separation of variables.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.4

concepts.name: Electron Spin and the Stern-Gerlach Experiment
concepts.description: Electron spin is an intrinsic quantum number with half-integer values, demonstrated by spatial quantisation in the Stern-Gerlach experiment.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.4

concepts.name: Pauli Exclusion Principle
concepts.description: The Pauli exclusion principle states that no two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously.

concepts.name: Quantum Tunneling
concepts.description: Quantum tunneling is the penetration of a particle through a potential barrier even when its energy is less than the barrier height.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.2

concepts.name: Time-Independent Perturbation Theory
concepts.description: Perturbation theory approximates energy corrections and eigenstate mixing when a small perturbation is added to a solvable Hamiltonian.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.6

concepts.name: Selection Rules and Transition Probabilities
concepts.description: Selection rules restrict which quantum transitions are allowed; they arise from conservation laws and matrix element calculations.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.11

concepts.name: Variational Method and Variational Principle
concepts.description: The variational principle states that for any normalised trial state |ψ⟩, the expectation value ⟨ψ|H|ψ⟩ ≥ E₀ (ground-state energy); minimising over a parametric family of trial wavefunctions gives an upper bound on E₀. The helium atom ground state is the canonical worked example.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.7

concepts.name: WKB Approximation (Semiclassical Limit)
concepts.description: The WKB approximation applies when the potential varies slowly on the scale of the de Broglie wavelength; it gives ψ ∝ exp(±i∫p dx/ℏ) in classically allowed regions and exponential decay in forbidden regions, recovering the exact Gamow tunneling formula.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.9

concepts.name: Identical Particles and the Symmetrization Postulate
concepts.description: The symmetrization postulate requires multi-particle wavefunctions to be symmetric under particle exchange (bosons) or antisymmetric (fermions); the Slater determinant constructs antisymmetric states and the spin-statistics theorem ties spin to exchange symmetry.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.5

concepts.name: Addition of Angular Momenta and Clebsch-Gordan Coefficients
concepts.description: When two angular momenta j₁ and j₂ are combined, the total J ranges from |j₁−j₂| to j₁+j₂; Clebsch-Gordan coefficients ⟨j₁m₁j₂m₂|JM⟩ give the change-of-basis between the uncoupled {m₁,m₂} and coupled {J,M} representations.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.4 §4.4

concepts.name: Scattering Theory and the Born Approximation
concepts.description: Scattering theory describes how particles scatter off a potential V; the Lippmann-Schwinger equation |ψ⟩ = |φ⟩ + G₀V|ψ⟩ generates the Born series, whose first term gives the differential cross-section dσ/dΩ ∝ |Ṽ(q)|² in terms of the Fourier transform of the potential.
references: Griffiths Introduction to Quantum Mechanics Ch.11

concepts.name: S-Matrix and Scattering Amplitudes
concepts.description: The S-matrix (scattering matrix) maps in-states to out-states: S = 1 + 2πiT; unitarity of S encodes conservation of probability. The T-matrix elements give scattering amplitudes, and the optical theorem relates Im f(θ=0) to the total cross-section.
references: Weinberg Lectures on Quantum Mechanics Ch.8

concepts.name: Density Matrix and Mixed Quantum States
concepts.description: The density operator ρ = Σᵢpᵢ|ψᵢ⟩⟨ψᵢ| represents statistical mixtures of quantum states; pure states satisfy ρ² = ρ while mixed states have Tr(ρ²) < 1. The von Neumann equation iℏ∂ρ/∂t = [H,ρ] governs time evolution.
references: Weinberg Lectures on Quantum Mechanics Ch.2

concepts.name: Postulates of Special Relativity
concepts.description: Einstein's two postulates are that the laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames and the speed of light is constant.

concepts.name: Relativity of Simultaneity
concepts.description: Events that are simultaneous in one inertial frame may not be simultaneous in another frame moving relative to it.

concepts.name: Time Dilation
concepts.description: A moving clock runs slower than a stationary one; the dilated time Δt = γΔτ where γ = 1/√(1−v²/c²).

concepts.name: Length Contraction
concepts.description: A moving object is measured to be shorter along the direction of motion by the factor 1/γ relative to its rest length.

concepts.name: Lorentz Transformations
concepts.description: The Lorentz transformations relate spacetime coordinates of events measured in two inertial frames in relative motion.

concepts.name: Relativistic Momentum and Energy
concepts.description: Relativistic momentum is p = γmv and total energy E = γmc²; together they form the energy-momentum four-vector.

concepts.name: Mass-Energy Equivalence: E=mc²
concepts.description: Mass-energy equivalence E = mc² states that mass and energy are interconvertible, with rest mass representing stored energy.

concepts.name: Spacetime Interval and Four-Vectors
concepts.description: The spacetime interval s² = c²t² − x² is invariant under Lorentz transformations; four-vectors generalise this to all relativistic quantities.

concepts.name: Probability Distributions in Physics
concepts.description: Statistical mechanics describes macroscopic behaviour through probability distributions over microscopic states.

concepts.name: Boltzmann Factor and Statistical Weight
concepts.description: The Boltzmann factor e^(−E/kT) gives the relative probability of a microstate at energy E in thermal equilibrium at temperature T.

concepts.name: Partition Function
concepts.description: The partition function Z = Σe^(−E_i/kT) sums Boltzmann factors over all microstates and encodes all thermodynamic information.

concepts.name: Maxwell-Boltzmann Speed Distribution
concepts.description: The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution gives the probability distribution of molecular speeds in an ideal gas in equilibrium.

concepts.name: Fermi-Dirac Statistics
concepts.description: Fermi-Dirac statistics describe the thermal occupation of quantum states by fermions, with the Fermi energy as the chemical potential at T=0.
references: Kittel Introduction to Solid State Physics

concepts.name: Bose-Einstein Statistics
concepts.description: Bose-Einstein statistics describe bosons that can occupy the same quantum state; at low temperatures they condense into the ground state.

concepts.name: Statistical Definition of Entropy
concepts.description: Boltzmann's formula S = k ln Ω defines entropy as the logarithm of the number of accessible microstates, linking statistical and thermodynamic entropy.

concepts.name: Helmholtz and Gibbs Free Energy
concepts.description: Helmholtz free energy F = U − TS and Gibbs free energy G = H − TS are thermodynamic potentials that determine equilibrium under different constraints.

concepts.name: Stellar Structure and the Main Sequence
concepts.description: Stars are self-gravitating plasma balls in hydrostatic equilibrium whose energy source is core nuclear fusion.

concepts.name: Stellar Evolution and End States
concepts.description: Stars evolve from the main sequence through red giant, and end as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes depending on their mass.

concepts.name: Big Bang Cosmology
concepts.description: Big Bang cosmology describes the origin and evolution of the universe from a hot, dense initial state through expansion and cooling.

concepts.name: Dark Matter and Dark Energy
concepts.description: Dark matter and dark energy are inferred from gravitational and cosmological observations but their physical nature remains unknown.

concepts.name: Black Holes and Schwarzschild Radius
concepts.description: A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape from within the Schwarzschild radius.

concepts.name: Gravitational Waves
concepts.description: Gravitational waves are ripples in spacetime caused by accelerating massive objects, first directly detected by LIGO in 2015.
